edges.description: Degeneration of central RPE and photoreceptors produces RPE atrophy.
Photoreceptor degeneration causes reduced ERG amplitudes and delayed responses.
Choriocapillaris atrophy contributes directly to the areolar chorioretinal atrophic lesion.
Central macular geographic atrophy is the structural basis for progressive macular degeneration.
Central macular atrophy causes progressive loss of central visual acuity.
The fovea-centered atrophic lesion produces a central visual field defect.

nodes.description: Full-field ERG abnormalities in PRPH2-associated disease, including reduced amplitudes and delayed peak times, reflecting generalized photoreceptor dysfunction beyond the visible macular lesion.

evidence.snippet: ERG showed a significantly reduced amplitude across all components (P < 0.001) and a peak time delay in the light-adapted 30-Hz flicker and single-flash b-wave (P < 0.001).

nodes.description: The end-stage, sharply bordered ("areolar") geographic atrophic lesion of the central macula encompassing RPE, choriocapillaris, and photoreceptor loss, causing irreversible central visual field loss while sparing the peripheral retina.

evidence.snippet: Central areolar choroidal dystrophy (CACD) is a rare hereditary disease that mainly affects the macula, resulting in progressive and usually profound visual loss.

nodes.description: Well-demarcated bilateral atrophy of the RPE and choriocapillaris centered on the fovea, the defining lesion of CACD.

evidence.snippet: Central areolar choroidal dystrophy is an inherited disorder characterized by progressive choriocapillaris atrophy and retinal degeneration and is usually associated with mutations in the PRPH2 gene.
diffuse RPE atrophy consistent with central areolar chorioretinal dystrophy (CACD) in the fifth decade of life

nodes.description: Central visual field defect corresponding to the atrophic lesion.

evidence.snippet: HVF revealed central scotoma in 6 of 7 eyes (85.7%), whereas a paracentral scotoma extending to fixation point was detected in 1 eye.

nodes.description: Secondary atrophy of the choriocapillaris and underlying choroidal vasculature beneath the degenerating central RPE, producing the characteristic well-demarcated areolar lesion of the central macula. OCT angiography can show choriocapillaris flow deficits extending beyond the clinically atrophic RPE.

evidence.snippet: Central areolar choroidal dystrophy is an inherited disorder characterized by progressive choriocapillaris atrophy and retinal degeneration and is usually associated with mutations in the PRPH2 gene.

nodes.description: Progressive degeneration of the central macula.

evidence.snippet: Central areolar choroidal dystrophy (CACD) is a rare hereditary disease that mainly affects the macula, resulting in progressive and usually profound visual loss.

nodes.description: Secondary microglial activation, reactive gliosis, and aberrant synaptic remodeling accompany photoreceptor degeneration, as demonstrated in the PRPH2 knock-in CACD mouse model. This neuroinflammatory response is a downstream amplifier of retinal degeneration rather than the primary lesion.

evidence.snippet: Prph2 knock-in mice recapitulate human central areolar choroidal dystrophy retinal degeneration and exhibit aberrant synaptic remodeling and microglial activation.

nodes.description: Pathogenic PRPH2 (peripherin-2) variants disrupt the photoreceptor outer-segment disc rim. Peripherin-2 is a tetraspanin that oligomerizes with ROM1 to maintain disc morphogenesis and rim curvature; defective outer-segment structure compromises rod and cone photoreceptor homeostasis in the central macula. This is the initiating lesion in the most common (CACD3) form.

evidence.snippet: They are functionally related to photoreceptors (either in the phototransduction process, as in the case of GUCY2D, or the recovery of retinal photodegradation in photoreceptors for GUCA1A, or the formation and maintenance of specific structures within photoreceptors for PRPH2).

nodes.description: In the GUCY2D (CACD2) and GUCA1A CACD-spectrum forms, the primary defect is in the calcium-dependent recovery phase of phototransduction. GUCY2D encodes retinal guanylate cyclase-1 (retGC1) and GUCA1A encodes its calcium sensor GCAP1; pathogenic variants dysregulate cGMP/Ca2+ homeostasis in photoreceptors, producing photoreceptor stress and degeneration that converges on the same macular RPE/choriocapillaris atrophy.

evidence.snippet: The mutation was shown in zebrafish to cause significant disruptions in photoreceptors and retinal pigment epithelium, together with atrophies of retinal vessels and choriocapillaris.

nodes.description: Progressive dysfunction and loss of the central macular retinal pigment epithelium and overlying photoreceptors, the primary degenerative event in CACD, preceding clinically apparent choriocapillaris loss. Reported CACD-causing genes are photoreceptor-expressed, supporting a photoreceptor/RPE-first cascade.

evidence.snippet: We corroborated the alteration of the outer segment structure, and we found changes in the synaptic connectivity in the outer plexiform layer as well as gliosis and signs of microglial activation.

nodes.description: Progressive loss of central visual acuity in mid-adult life.

evidence.snippet: Central areolar choroidal dystrophy (CACD) is a rare hereditary disease that mainly affects the macula, resulting in progressive and usually profound visual loss.

nodes.description: Atrophy of the central macular retinal pigment epithelium.

evidence.snippet: diffuse RPE atrophy consistent with central areolar chorioretinal dystrophy (CACD) in the fifth decade of life
